one_line_takeaway: A unified benchmark of 15 LLM input guardrails shows detection quality swings wildly by attack style and that cheap fine-tuned classifiers (notably DeBERTa) match or beat expensive LLM-based defences -- especially out-of-distribution -- exposing that current academic datasets are too narrow to prove LLM guardrails generalise better.
key_finding: Detection performance varies dramatically by attack style, so evaluating on a broad dataset spread is essential. The authors' simple fine-tuned transformer classifiers dominate on their own metrics: DeBERTa reaches AUC 0.996 / ACC 0.991 / F1 0.97 on the in-distribution test set and still leads on OOD (AUC 0.976), outperforming heavier open/closed guardrails. The Random Forest is strong in-distribution (AUC 0.99, ACC 0.938) but generalises poorly OOD (F1 drops to 0.565). LLM-based judges raise detection but inflate false positives non-uniformly (e.g., SmoothLLM spikes FP on BoolQ and XSTest, an effect its original paper missed). ProtectAI v2 underperforms badly (in-distribution AUC 0.569, F1 0.348). Granite Guardian 3.0 is the strongest LLM judge (in-distribution F1 0.911; combined-set F1 0.821). The authors draw a dilemma: either open data already captures attack diversity (so cheap classifiers suffice) or current academic datasets are too thin to demonstrate any generalisation advantage of expensive LLM defences. Practically, SmoothLLM can add up to 10 extra inferences and NeMo 1 per prompt, and memory ranges from 91 MB (LangKit) to 26.03 GB (Vicuna-13b), making order-of-magnitude compute increases hard to justify over lightweight classifiers.
core_contribution: The paper (IBM Research, NeurIPS 2024 Safe Generative AI Workshop) is a systematic, apples-to-apples benchmark of 15 input-side jailbreak/prompt-injection defences ('guardrails') for LLMs, evaluated over a large compiled suite of malicious and benign datasets under a single unified evaluation framework. Its own contributions are: (I) exposing limitations of prior guardrail evaluations that use narrow attack sets and inconsistent metrics; (II) systematically measuring attack detection across many attack styles with combined metrics; (III) evaluating 15 defences on a common in-distribution and out-of-distribution (OOD) split; and (IV) analysing whether defence model complexity yields better OOD generalisation. The central empirical claim -- and the paper's own result, not cited work -- is that simple, cheap classifier baselines the authors train themselves (Random Forest on unigrams, and fine-tuned BERT/DeBERTa/GPT-2) are competitive with, and on OOD data can surpass, far more expensive state-of-the-art open- and closed-source LLM-based guardrails.
threat_model: Adversary submits adversarial input prompts (jailbreaks and prompt injections) to an LLM-integrated application to elicit unsafe/adversarially-controlled outputs, leak confidential information, or override system instructions. Attack vectors span manually crafted role-playing/DAN prompts, optimization-based attacks (e.g., GCG adversarial suffixes), model-generated 'artificial' attacks (AutoDAN, SAP, AART), and instruction-override/prompt-extraction attacks. The defender deploys an external input guardrail that vets each prompt before it reaches the main LLM and classifies it as malicious vs benign. The paper frames evaluation around a realistic deployment concern: a defence must catch out-of-distribution/novel attacks while keeping the false-positive rate on benign traffic low enough for production use.
limitations: By the authors' own statement, the final evaluation data had to be sub-sampled because several defences are computationally expensive, so results reflect a subset rather than full datasets. The benchmark is input-side only (no output-filtering guardrails). Their own classifiers benefit from being trained on the in-distribution portion, so the in-distribution comparison is not fully symmetric with off-the-shelf defences (the authors flag that the ID/OOD split only applies to models they trained; for others they report a combined set in Table 4). Some proprietary components are opaque (LangKit's malicious embedding prompts and one dataset, IBM Prompt Extraction, are unspecified/internal). GCG true-positive numbers were computed against Vicuna-13b specifically. The work does not evaluate adaptive attackers who craft prompts against the guardrails themselves, and 'artificial'/model-generated attacks may favour classifier memorisation.

datasets_used: In-distribution evaluation set: 11387 sampled instances (9543 benign, 1844 malicious)
quantitative_results: DeBERTa in-distribution: AUC 0.996, ACC 0.991, F1 0.97, Recall 0.963, Precision 0.978 (best overall in-distribution)
GPT-2 classifier in-distribution: AUC 0.994, ACC 0.974, F1 0.916
Random Forest in-distribution: AUC 0.99, ACC 0.938, F1 0.762, Recall 0.618, Precision 0.995
Random Forest OOD: AUC 0.951, ACC 0.979, F1 0.565 (poor OOD generalisation)
DeBERTa OOD: AUC 0.976, ACC 0.969, F1 0.672, Recall 0.91
GPT-2 OOD: AUC 0.969, ACC 0.979, F1 0.72 (best OOD F1)
ProtectAI v2 in-distribution: AUC 0.569, ACC 0.868, F1 0.348, Recall 0.217 (weakest)
Llama-Guard 2 in-distribution: ACC 0.95, F1 0.826; OOD F1 0.474; combined ACC 0.955, F1 0.758
Granite Guardian 3.0 in-distribution: ACC 0.971, F1 0.911; OOD F1 0.560, Recall 0.944; combined ACC 0.960, F1 0.821 (best LLM judge)
SmoothLLM in-distribution: ACC 0.833, F1 0.591, Precision 0.490; OOD F1 0.163 (high FP, e.g., spikes on BoolQ/XSTest)
Perplexity filter in-distribution: ACC 0.771, F1 0.114; OOD F1 0.001 (near-useless OOD)
OpenAI Moderation (text-moderation-007) in-distribution: AUC 0.864, F1 0.291; OOD AUC 0.905, F1 0.134
Azure AI Content Safety: ACC 0.983, F1 0.703 (same in-distribution and OOD rows)
Memory footprint range: LangKit Injection Detection 91 MB to Vicuna-13b 26.03 GB; BERT/DeBERTa/GPT-2 transformers 371-548 MB
Extra inference calls: SmoothLLM up to 10 per prompt; NeMo input rail 1 per prompt
baselines_compared: Random Forest (unigram features) - authors' simple baseline
Perplexity threshold filter (Jain et al., GPT-2 based)
